note_data.title: Incomplete Records (AS6)
keyPoints: Financial statements provide information about financial position, financial performance and cash flows useful to users in making economic decisions
Manufacturing account is prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
Manufacturing account only includes information about the factory and actual manufacturing process
Cost of production is calculated by adding factory overheads to prime cost
Prime cost (direct cost) is the total of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses
Factory overheads (indirect costs) include costs from running the factory such as indirect factory wages and depreciation of factory machinery
Transfer price is the production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Manufacturing profit is the difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods
Work in progress represents inventory of partly finished goods in the factory at any point in time

definitions.definition: An account prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
The total of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses
Costs incurred from the running of the factory including indirect factory wages and depreciation of factory machinery
The production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
The difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods
Inventory of partly finished goods in the factory at any point in time

howToAnswer.instruction: Show all workings clearly. Use control account format for debtors/creditors calculations.
Use proper headings and format. For Statement of Affairs, list assets and liabilities systematically.
Show the method used (e.g., capital comparison) and all adjustments made.

modelAnswer.question: Fatima's business has incomplete records. Opening capital was Rs.50,000. During the year she withdrew Rs.15,000 and introduced additional capital of Rs.8,000. Closing capital was Rs.65,000. Calculate the profit for the year.
modelAnswer.answer:
Capital comparison method:
Closing capital Rs.65,000
Less: Opening capital Rs.50,000
Increase in capital Rs.15,000
Add: Drawings Rs.15,000
Less: Additional capital Rs.8,000
Profit for the year Rs.22,000
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Correct adjustments for drawings and additional capital

commonMistakes: Including non-production costs like administration, finance, and distribution costs in the manufacturing account instead of the income statement
Confusing prime cost with total production cost - prime cost excludes factory overheads
Mixing up direct costs and indirect costs when calculating manufacturing costs
examinerTips: Remember that manufacturing account only deals with factory costs - all other costs go to the income statement
Use the formula: Cost of production = prime cost + factory overheads
formulas: Profit = Closing Capital - Opening Capital + Drawings - Additional Capital
Sales = Opening Debtors + Credit Sales - Cash Received from Customers
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Statement of Affairs format showing Fixed Assets, Current Assets, Current Liabilities, Long-term Liabilities, and Capital calculation. Control account T-format showing opening balance, additions, deductions, and closing balance.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to calculate opening capital using bank records and additional information about drawings and profit
N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) tested preparation of a trading account from incomplete records, requiring students to calculate purchases using creditors' information
M/J 2022 P1 Q5 (4 marks) focused on calculating closing capital using the accounting equation after providing partial balance sheet information
N 2021 P2 Q2 (10 marks) required students to prepare a complete income statement and balance sheet from cash book and additional details about accruals and prepayments

workedExample.question: John's business records were destroyed in a fire. The following information is available: Bank balance 1 Jan: $2,400, Bank balance 31 Dec: $3,100, Cash received from customers: $15,600, Payments to suppliers: $8,900, Drawings: $2,200, Other expenses paid: $1,800. Calculate the profit for the year. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate net change in bank balance = $3,100 - $2,400 = $700 increase
Step 2: List all receipts = Cash from customers $15,600
Step 3: List all payments = Suppliers $8,900 + Other expenses $1,800 + Drawings $2,200 = $12,900
Step 4: Apply cash flow formula: Opening balance + Receipts - Payments = Closing balance
Step 5: $2,400 + $15,600 - $12,900 = $5,100 (this should equal closing balance but we have $3,100)
Step 6: The difference represents profit taken out: Profit = Receipts - Business payments = $15,600 - ($8,900 + $1,800) = $4,900
workedExample.answer: Profit for the year = $4,900. This can be verified as the business generated $4,900 profit, of which $2,200 was taken as drawings and $2,700 remained in the business (explaining the $700 bank increase plus $2,000 difference).
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for identifying receipts, 2 marks for calculating total payments, 2 marks for correct application of accounting equation or cash flow method, 1 mark for final answer

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused drawings with business expenses when calculating profit' — always exclude personal drawings from business expense calculations

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to account for opening and closing inventory when calculating purchases' — always use the formula: Purchases = Cost of sales + Closing inventory - Opening inventory

Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often failed to distinguish between cash and credit transactions when reconstructing accounts' — clearly separate cash sales/purchases from credit sales/purchases using debtor and creditor movements
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw T-accounts showing the reconstruction process. Essential diagram shows: (1) Bank/Cash T-account with all known receipts and payments, balancing figure representing unknown item, (2) Debtors T-account showing opening balance, credit sales (balancing figure), cash received, and closing balance, (3) Creditors T-account showing opening balance, purchases (balancing figure), cash paid, and closing balance. Include arrows showing the flow from T-accounts to Trading and P&L account preparation.
note_data.quickRecap: Manufacturing accounts calculate the production cost of manufactured goods by combining prime costs (direct materials, labour, expenses) with factory overheads. The manufacturing account focuses solely on factory operations, while other business costs appear in the income statement. Transfer pricing involves adding a mark-up to production costs, creating manufacturing profit.
